passages.explanation: The Kingdom of Heaven is like a landowner who goes out early in the morning to hire labourers for his vineyard. He agrees on a denarius for the day. He goes out again at the third hour, the sixth, the ninth, and the eleventh — hiring workers each time. When evening comes, he pays everyone the same — one denarius, starting with the last hired. Those who worked all day grumble: these last worked one hour, and you've made them equal to us who bore the burden and the scorching heat! The landowner replies: friend, I'm doing you no wrong. Didn't you agree for a denarius? Take what's yours and go. I choose to give to this last the same as to you. Isn't it lawful for me to do what I want with what I own? Or is your eye evil because I am good? So the last will be first, and the first last. The parable isn't about fairness in employment — it's about grace. Nobody earned the kingdom. Those who think they did will resent those who clearly didn't.

As Jesus goes up to Jerusalem, he takes the twelve disciples aside and tells them: we're going up to Jerusalem. The Son of Man will be delivered to the chief priests and scribes. They will condemn him to death and hand him over to the Gentiles to mock, scourge, and crucify. On the third day he will be raised up. This is the third and most detailed passion prediction. Jesus now names the method — crucifixion — and specifies Gentile involvement. He walks toward it with full knowledge.

The mother of James and John comes with her sons, kneeling and making a request: command that these two sons of mine may sit, one at your right hand and one at your left, in your kingdom. Jesus asks them: can you drink the cup I'm about to drink? They say: we can. He tells them: you'll indeed drink my cup, but sitting at my right and left isn't mine to give — it's for those for whom my Father has prepared it. The other ten are indignant. Jesus calls them together: you know that the rulers of the nations lord it over them, and their great ones exercise authority. It won't be so among you. Whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant. Whoever wants to be first must be your slave — just as the Son of Man didn't come to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many. Immediately after the third passion prediction, the disciples are jockeying for position. They hear cross and think throne. Jesus redefines greatness as service.

As they leave Jericho, a great multitude follows. Two blind men sitting by the roadside hear that Jesus is passing by and cry out: Lord, have mercy on us, son of David! The multitude rebukes them, telling them to be quiet. They cry out louder: Lord, have mercy on us, son of David! Jesus stands still, calls them, and asks: what do you want me to do for you? They say: Lord, that our eyes may be opened. Moved with compassion, Jesus touches their eyes. Immediately they receive their sight and follow him. The crowd tries to silence them. Desperation doesn't care about social expectations. They get louder, and Jesus stops everything for them.
